summary: Anglo-Irish 18th-century author known for the play She Stoops to Conquer, the novel The Vicar of Wakefield, and the poem 'The Deserted Village.' Associated with Samuel Johnson's circle.

works.name: She Stoops to Conquer
works.description: Goldsmith's most famous play, a 'Laughing Comedy' with a prologue by David Garrick attacking sentimental comedy. Tony Lumpkin pranks Charles Marlow and George Hastings by directing them to his family's manor and telling them it's an inn. The shy Marlow is attracted to Kate Hardcastle only when she disguises herself as a barmaid. Tony drives his mother around the house in circles and dumps her in a horsepond. The song 'Let schoolmasters puzzle their brain' is sung at the Three Pigeons alehouse. Kate has an arrangement with her father to dress differently at different times of day.

clues.clue: Kate Hardcastle disguises as a barmaid to woo the shy Marlow

works.name: The Deserted Village
works.description: A 1770 poem in heroic couplets lamenting the depopulation of 'Sweet Auburn.' Contains the famous lines 'Ill fares the land, to hastening ills a prey / Where wealth accumulates, and men decay' (which titles Tony Judt's book). Describes the loss of 'light labour,' 'the rural virtues,' and a 'bold peasantry.' An attack on the enclosure movement. Samuel Johnson contributed the last two couplets. George Crabbe wrote The Village in response.

works.name: The Vicar of Wakefield
works.description: A novel about Dr. Charles Primrose, whose family loses and regains their fortune. Sophia is rescued from drowning by Mr. Burchell. Olivia fakes her death and obtains a marriage license to Squire Thornhill.

clues.clue: Dr. Charles Primrose — vicar whose family loses and regains fortune
examples: Dr. Primrose's family loses and regains their fortune

clues.clue: Sophia rescued from drowning by Mr. Burchell; Olivia and Squire Thornhill
examples: Sophia being rescued from drowning by Mr. Burchell

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Anglo-Irish author. Friend of Samuel Johnson — Boswell records a conversation where Goldsmith told Johnson a fable about fishes wanting to be birds. Wrote The Citizen of the World (letters from Chinese traveler Lien Chi). Also wrote the comedy The Good Natur'd Man. Austen's History of England parodies Goldsmith's own History of England. May be the origin of the phrase 'goody two-shoes.'

examples: told Johnson a fable about fishes wanting to become birds

clues.clue: Austen parodied his History of England
examples: Austen's History of England parodies this writer's own History of England

comprehensive_summary:
Oliver Goldsmith (c. 1728-1774) was an Anglo-Irish author whose works span poetry, drama, and fiction. In quizbowl, he is most frequently clued for his play She Stoops to Conquer, in which Tony Lumpkin tricks the shy Charles Marlow and George Hastings into believing his family's manor is an inn. Kate Hardcastle disguises herself as a barmaid to attract the shy Marlow. Tony drives his mother around the house in circles and dumps her in a horsepond. The play has a prologue by David Garrick attacking sentimental comedy and is classified as a 'Laughing Comedy.'

'The Deserted Village' is a 1770 poem in heroic couplets lamenting the depopulation of 'Sweet Auburn' and containing the famous lines 'Ill fares the land, to hastening ills a prey / Where wealth accumulates, and men decay.' It is an attack on the enclosure movement. Samuel Johnson contributed the last two couplets, and George Crabbe wrote The Village in pessimistic response.

The Vicar of Wakefield follows Dr. Charles Primrose's family through loss and recovery of fortune. Goldsmith was part of Samuel Johnson's circle, and Boswell records their conversations. He also wrote The Citizen of the World and The Good Natur'd Man.
